summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on his first experience adopting formal unit testing with NUnit, finding the implementation straightforward but the harder challenge being what and how to test. He argues that even basic unit tests are valuable because they force developers to systematically think about testing rather than relying on ad-hoc clicking around. However, he raises a practical tension: aggressive refactoring in early development stages creates a pile of failing tests, potentially discouraging either good testing practices or necessary design changes. He questions whether the test-first approach actually helps or hinders this balance.
key_points: Unit testing implementation is simple; the real challenge is determining what makes a good test
Most developers don't test at all—they just click around and ship if there are no exceptions
The true value of unit testing is forcing developers to systematically consider testing questions about their code
Early-stage refactoring creates tension with unit testing since design changes break existing tests
Test-first development may conflict with the reality of how projects evolve through major design iterations
money_quotes: The problem with unit testing, then, is not the implementation. It's determining what to test. And how to test it.
Most developers don't test at all! They key in a few values at random and click a few buttons. If they don't get any unhandled exceptions, that code is ready for QA!
The real value of unit testing is that it forces you to stop and think about testing.
Unit tests don't guarantee correct functioning of a program. I think it's unreasonable to expect them to.
Writing unit tests does guarantee that the developer has considered, however briefly, these truly difficult testing questions.

My main problem at the moment with unit tests is when I change a design I get a stack of failing tests. This means I'm either going to write less tests or make fewer big design changes. Both of which are bad things.
key_insight: Unit testing's greatest value isn't catching bugs—it's forcing developers to systematically think through testing questions they would otherwise ignore.
